title: 💰 Sole Proprietor vs LLC vs S Corp: Real Math 2026
description:
Sole proprietor, LLC, or S corp, what saves more in 2026? Real numbers, real thresholds, and the formation costs that change the answer for US freelancers, plus UK sole trader vs limited company.

Personal finance, business entity choice, LLC vs S corp, sole proprietor 2026.

segments.voice: Sole proprietor, single-member LLC, or S corporation. Picking the wrong business entity can cost a US freelancer five to ten thousand dollars a year in unnecessary tax. The IRS Statistics of Income data from two thousand twenty-three shows that over seventy percent of small businesses in the US are organized as sole proprietorships even when an S corp would save more money. Today, the real math.

Quick legal definitions first. Sole proprietorship, you and the business are legally the same. No paperwork to start, no filing fee, just file Schedule C with your 1040. Single-member LLC, a state-level legal structure that gives liability protection but defaults to sole proprietorship taxation. S corporation, a tax election made via Form 2553 that changes how the IRS taxes a business already structured as an LLC or corporation.

Liability layer first because it does not depend on tax math. As a sole proprietor, your personal assets are exposed to business creditors. Get sued and your house and savings can be on the table. An LLC, properly maintained, separates your personal and business assets per state corporation law. The cost is fifty to five hundred dollars in state filing fees plus an annual report fee in most states.
segments.visual: liability protection diagram sole prop LLC

segments.voice: Now the tax side. Sole prop and single-member LLC are taxed identically by default, both file Schedule C and pay self-employment tax of fifteen point three percent on net earnings up to the Social Security wage base. So at this level, the LLC is purely a liability shield, no tax benefit. Many people misunderstand this and form an LLC expecting magical tax savings, then discover the bill looks identical.
segments.visual: schedule C tax form sole prop LLC

segments.voice: Here is where S corp election changes everything. By filing Form 2553 with the IRS, an LLC can elect to be taxed as an S corporation. Under S corp rules, the owner pays themselves a reasonable salary subject to FICA, then takes the rest of the profit as distributions, which are not subject to FICA. This creates the SE tax savings that drive the strategy.

A worked example. A freelance consultant nets one hundred twenty thousand dollars in profit. As a sole prop, the SE tax is roughly sixteen thousand seven hundred dollars on the full amount up to the wage base. As an S corp paying sixty thousand reasonable salary, FICA is paid only on that sixty, about nine thousand one hundred eighty. The remaining sixty thousand comes out as distribution with zero SE tax. Savings, around seven thousand five hundred dollars.
segments.visual: tax savings comparison S corp example

segments.voice: But S corp has overhead. You need to run actual payroll, which costs around five hundred dollars a year through Gusto or OnPay. You file a corporate tax return Form 1120-S, which usually costs eight hundred to fifteen hundred at a CPA. State franchise taxes add anywhere from zero to eight hundred a year depending on the state. Total overhead, fifteen hundred to twenty-five hundred annually.

The breakeven. Below about eighty thousand in net profit, S corp overhead usually eats the savings. Between eighty and one hundred fifty thousand, S corp typically saves three to seven thousand a year net. Above one hundred fifty thousand, the savings climb but plateau because FICA caps at the Social Security wage base of one hundred seventy-eight thousand for two thousand twenty-six.
segments.visual: breakeven graph profit S corp savings

segments.voice: Reasonable compensation, the IRS audit trigger. Pay yourself too little salary and the IRS will reclassify distributions back to wages with penalties. Per IRS guidance and Tax Court cases like Watson v Commissioner, the salary needs to reflect the work you actually do. RCReports and other compensation databases help you document a defensible figure. A solo software developer might justify sixty to eighty thousand on a one hundred fifty thousand profit.

C corporation, mostly the wrong answer for solos. C corps face double taxation, profits taxed at the twenty-one percent corporate rate then again as dividends to shareholders. The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act dropped the corporate rate to a flat twenty-one percent in twenty seventeen, which made C corps slightly more attractive for retained earnings, but the double taxation still bites for any profit you actually want to take out.

When does C corp make sense. If you are reinvesting all profits back into the business and not taking salary or dividends, retained earnings grow at the twenty-one percent rate which can be lower than your personal bracket. Also, qualified small business stock under IRC section 1202 lets founders exclude up to ten million dollars of gain on sale after a five-year hold, a major exit advantage that does not exist for LLCs.

Partnership and multi-member LLC. If you have a partner, you default to partnership taxation, filing Form 1065 with K-1s issued to each partner. Each partner pays SE tax on their share. Partnerships can also elect S corp status if they meet the requirements, but partners with materially different roles need careful structuring around reasonable compensation rules.

State considerations. Some states tax LLCs and S corps differently. California charges an eight hundred dollar minimum franchise tax on every LLC regardless of profit, plus a gross receipts fee above two hundred fifty thousand. Texas has the franchise tax. New York has its own corporate filing fees. Always check your state filing fees and ongoing taxes before forming, the federal math is only half the picture.

UK readers, your equivalent decision. Sole trader is the simplest, register with HMRC and file Self Assessment annually, pay income tax and Class 4 NI on profits. Limited company is the equivalent of a US corporation, pays nineteen percent corporation tax on profits up to fifty thousand and twenty-five percent above two hundred fifty thousand for two thousand twenty-six per HMRC guidance.
segments.visual: UK sole trader vs limited company

segments.voice: The UK salary plus dividend split. Limited company directors typically pay themselves a small salary up to the personal allowance, twelve thousand five hundred seventy pounds for two thousand twenty-six, then take the rest as dividends. Dividends face dividend tax at eight point seventy-five, thirty-three point seventy-five, or thirty-nine point thirty-five percent, lower than income tax for higher earners. The math is structurally similar to the US S corp split.

Disregarded entity rules in the US. A single-member LLC is a disregarded entity by default, meaning the IRS ignores it and taxes you directly as if it were a sole prop. The LLC still gives state-level liability protection, but the federal tax form is Schedule C, the same as a sole prop. Adding a member or electing S corp status changes that classification.

Switching mid-year. The S corp election generally needs to be made by the fifteenth day of the third month of the tax year, March fifteenth for calendar year filers. Late elections are allowed under Rev Proc 2013-30 if you have reasonable cause and file within three years and seventy-five days. Many CPAs help backdate elections successfully when the timing was missed.

Setting up payroll for S corp. Once elected, you must run actual payroll for yourself. Gusto, OnPay, and Patriot Payroll handle this for around forty dollars a month. The system withholds federal and state taxes from each paycheck, files quarterly 941 forms, and issues W-2s at year end. Without proper payroll, the IRS can collapse the S corp election entirely.

Health insurance through S corp. The owner can deduct premiums above the line, but the corporation has to include the premiums in W-2 Box 1 wages. This sounds confusing but it works out, you avoid the SE tax on health premiums while still deducting them. The CPA who set up your S corp should handle this properly during year-end W-2 preparation.
segments.visual: health insurance S corp owner deduction

segments.voice: Solo 401(k) interaction. With S corp, your retirement contributions split between salary deferrals from the W-2 and employer contributions from the corporation. The total combined limit for two thousand twenty-six is seventy thousand, plus seventy-five hundred catch-up at fifty per IRS Notice 2025-67. The S corp owner can max retirement aggressively while still capturing the FICA savings on distributions.

Common S corp mistakes. Failing to run real payroll, paying yourself unreasonably low salary, taking distributions before the salary obligation is met, mixing personal and business expenses in the corporate account. Each of these can collapse the S corp election in audit. The IRS has specific guidance in the Internal Revenue Manual section 4 dot 23 that auditors follow on these issues.
segments.visual: S corp common mistakes warning list

segments.voice: When to consult a professional. Below fifty thousand profit, file as a sole prop and skip the complexity. Between fifty and eighty thousand, single-member LLC for liability protection only. Above eighty thousand, get a CPA consultation on the S corp election. The fee for that consultation is typically two to four hundred dollars and almost always pays for itself many times over.

What this looks like long term. A freelancer earning one hundred fifty thousand who switches from sole prop to S corp at year three, properly structured, captures roughly seven thousand a year in SE tax savings. Over a fifteen year career, that is one hundred five thousand dollars saved, ignoring growth. Reinvested in an index fund at the historical seven percent real return, that compounds to over two hundred thousand.
